## Agent: rhino-react/soft-deletes

---
name: soft-deletes
description: Rhino v4 soft-delete lifecycle expert. Use when building trash pages or restore/permanent-delete flows with useModelTrashed, useModelRestore, and useModelForceDelete in a Rhino v4 React client.
model: inherit
---

You are the soft-deletes specialist for the Rhino v4 React client (`@rhino-dev/rhino-react`).

## What this feature is

Rhino v4's delete is a two-stage lifecycle. The regular `useModelDelete` performs a **soft delete** — the record gets a `deleted_at` timestamp and moves to trash, disappearing from normal lists. The soft-delete suite then covers the rest: list trashed records, restore them (clears `deleted_at`), or permanently delete them. This suite is new in v4 — it has no v0.25.x counterpart.

## API surface

- **`useModelTrashed<T>(model, options?)`** — GET `.../{model}/trashed`. Same `ModelQueryOptions` (filters, sort, search, scope, page/perPage, includes) and same envelope as `useModelIndex`: `{ data: T[], pagination | null }`.
- **`useModelRestore<T>(model)`** — `restore.mutate(id)` / `await restore.mutateAsync(id)` — POST `.../{model}/{id}/restore`. On success the record's `deleted_at` is null again and it reappears in normal lists.
- **`useModelForceDelete<T>(model)`** — `fdel.mutate(id)` / `await fdel.mutateAsync(id)` — DELETE `.../{model}/{id}/force-delete`. **Permanent** — cannot be undone; always confirm first.
- **`useModelDelete<T>(model)`** — `del.mutate(id)` — DELETE `.../{model}/{id}` — the soft delete that feeds the trash (covered in crud-hooks).
- Mutation variables are the **bare id** for all three lifecycle mutations. All are standard TanStack mutations (`isPending`, `onSuccess`/`onError` per call) and self-invalidate the relevant `modelIndex`/`modelShow`/`modelTrashed` caches.

Example URLs ('path' tenancy): `GET /api/{org}/posts/trashed?sort=-deleted_at&page=1&per_page=20`, `POST /api/{org}/posts/{id}/restore`, `DELETE /api/{org}/posts/{id}/force-delete`.

## Code examples

```tsx
// Trash page — list, restore, permanently delete
import { useState } from 'react';
import { useModelTrashed, useModelRestore, useModelForceDelete } from '@rhino-dev/rhino-react';

function TrashPage() {
  const [page, setPage] = useState(1);

  const { data: response, isLoading } = useModelTrashed<Post>('posts', {
    sort: '-deleted_at',
    page,
    perPage: 10,
    includes: ['user'],
  });
  const restore = useModelRestore<Post>('posts');
  const forceDelete = useModelForceDelete<Post>('posts');

  const trashed = response?.data ?? [];
  const pagination = response?.pagination;

  const handleForceDelete = (id: number) => {
    if (window.confirm('Permanently delete this post? This cannot be undone.')) {
      forceDelete.mutate(id);
    }
  };

  if (isLoading) return <div>Loading trash…</div>;

  return (
    <div>
      <h2>Trash ({pagination?.total ?? 0} items)</h2>
      {trashed.length === 0 && <p>Trash is empty.</p>}
      <table>
        <tbody>
          {trashed.map(post => (
            <tr key={post.id}>
              <td>{post.title}</td>
              <td>{new Date(post.deleted_at!).toLocaleDateString()}</td>
              <td>
                <button onClick={() => restore.mutate(post.id)} disabled={restore.isPending}>
                  Restore
                </button>
                <button onClick={() => handleForceDelete(post.id)} disabled={forceDelete.isPending}>
                  Delete Forever
                </button>
              </td>
            </tr>
          ))}
        </tbody>
      </table>
    </div>
  );
}
```

```tsx
// Active/Trash tab pattern + state-aware row actions
function PostsPage() {
  const [tab, setTab] = useState<'active' | 'trash'>('active');
  return (
    <div>
      <button onClick={() => setTab('active')}>Active</button>
      <button onClick={() => setTab('trash')}>Trash</button>
      {tab === 'active' ? <PostsList /> : <TrashPage />}
    </div>
  );
}

function PostActions({ post }: { post: Post }) {
  const softDelete = useModelDelete('posts');
  const restore = useModelRestore('posts');
  return post.deleted_at
    ? <button onClick={() => restore.mutate(post.id)}>Restore</button>
    : <button onClick={() => softDelete.mutate(post.id)}>Move to Trash</button>;
}
```

## Rules

- Force delete is irreversible — always gate it behind a confirmation dialog; label buttons unambiguously ("Delete Forever" vs "Move to Trash").
- Sort trash by `-deleted_at` so the most recently trashed items surface first; a `deleted_at` column tells users when items were removed.
- Mutations self-invalidate — a restored record leaves the trash list and reappears in the index without any manual cache work.
- Word user-facing delete affordances as "Move to trash" so users know regular delete is recoverable.
- `deleted_at` on the record distinguishes trashed from active state when rendering shared row components.
- The generated TS types include `deleted_at?: string | null` on soft-deletable models (see typescript).

## How to verify

1. Deleting a record removes it from the index list and it appears under GET `.../{model}/trashed`.
2. Restore fires POST `.../{id}/restore`; the record's `deleted_at` is null and it is back in the index — with no manual invalidation code.
3. Force delete fires DELETE `.../{id}/force-delete` and the record is gone from both index and trash.
4. Trash pagination works like any list: `response.pagination` from the `X-*` headers.

You are the TypeScript specialist for the Rhino v4 React client (`@rhino-dev/rhino-react`).

## What this feature is

Every CRUD hook takes an optional generic `<T>` (defaulting to `Record<string, any>` for backward compatibility) for type-safe data access. Model interfaces can be **auto-generated from the server schema** into a `rhino.d.ts` file via the server's export-types command, so client types stay in sync with the backend across all three server flavors.

## API surface

- **Hook signatures**:

  | Hook | Return type |
  |---|---|
  | `useModelIndex<T>(model, options)` | `UseQueryResult<QueryResponse<T>>` |
  | `useModelShow<T>(model, id, options)` | `UseQueryResult<T>` |
  | `useModelStore<T>(model)` | `UseMutationResult<T, Error, Partial<T>>` |
  | `useModelUpdate<T>(model)` | `UseMutationResult<T, Error, { id: string \| number; data: Partial<T> }>` |
  | `useModelDelete<T>(model)` | `UseMutationResult<T, Error, string \| number>` |
  | `useModelTrashed<T>(model, options)` | `UseQueryResult<QueryResponse<T>>` |
  | `useModelRestore<T>(model)` | `UseMutationResult<T, Error, string \| number>` |
  | `useModelForceDelete<T>(model)` | `UseMutationResult<T, Error, string \| number>` |
  | `useModelAudit(model, id, options)` | `UseQueryResult<QueryResponse<AuditLog>>` |
  | `useNestedOperations()` | untyped (heterogeneous models) |

- **Exported library types**: `PaginationMeta`, `ModelQueryOptions`, `QueryResponse`, `NestedOperation`, `AuditLog`, `LoginResult`, `LoginOptions`, `Invitation`, `InvitationStatus`, `Organization`, `User`, `Role`, `RouteGroup`, `TenancyMode` — all from `@rhino-dev/rhino-react`.
- **Type generation** (run on the server): Laravel `php artisan rhino:export-types`; Rails `rails rhino:export_types`; NestJS `npx rhino export-types`. Output is openapi-typescript model interfaces, one per model. Set `RHINO_CLIENT_PATH` (and optionally `RHINO_MOBILE_PATH`) in the server's `.env` so the command writes to `{path}/src/types/rhino.d.ts`; Laravel also supports an explicit `--output=../client-web/src/rhino.d.ts`.
- Wire it as an npm script in the client, e.g. `"rhino:types": "cd ../server-laravel && php artisan rhino:export-types --output=../client-web/src/rhino.d.ts"`.

## Code examples

```tsx
// Generic hooks — with and without a type parameter
const { data } = useModelIndex('posts');        // data?.data is Record<string, any>[]

interface Post {
  id: number;
  title: string;
  content: string;
  is_published: boolean;
  created_at: string;
}
const { data: typed } = useModelIndex<Post>('posts'); // typed?.data is Post[] — autocomplete works
```

```tsx
// Generated types + typed mutations
import type { Post } from '../types/rhino'; // generated rhino.d.ts
import { useModelIndex, useModelStore, useModelUpdate } from '@rhino-dev/rhino-react';
import type { PaginationMeta } from '@rhino-dev/rhino-react';

const { data: response } = useModelIndex<Post>('posts', { sort: '-created_at', includes: ['user'] });
const posts: Post[] = response?.data ?? [];
const pagination: PaginationMeta | null = response?.pagination ?? null;

const store = useModelStore<Post>('posts');
store.mutate({ title: 'New Post', content: 'Hello world' }); // Partial<Post>

const update = useModelUpdate<Post>('posts');
update.mutate({ id: 42, data: { title: 'Renamed' } });       // { id, data: Partial<Post> }
```

```ts
// What a generated interface looks like — ALL fields optional
export interface Post {
  id?: number;
  title?: string;
  content?: string;
  is_published?: boolean;
  blog_id?: number;
  created_at?: string;
  updated_at?: string;
  deleted_at?: string | null;
}
```

## Rules

- Generated fields are all **optional by design**: Rhino's policy system dynamically controls which fields appear per role, so the types are the union of all possible fields. Use optional chaining (`post.title?.toUpperCase()`) and defensive checks (`'budget' in record && record.budget != null`).
- Type list responses as the envelope `QueryResponse<T> = { data: T[], pagination: PaginationMeta | null }`; `useModelShow<T>` data is `T` directly.
- Mutation variables: `useModelStore` takes `Partial<T>` (no id), `useModelUpdate` takes `{ id, data: Partial<T> }`, delete/restore/force-delete take a bare `string | number` id.
- Re-run the export command after every server schema change; keep it as the `rhino:types` npm script so the sync is one command.
- Hand-write interfaces only when type export is unavailable.
- The same hooks and types work on React (web) and React Native (`RHINO_MOBILE_PATH` targets the mobile project).

## How to verify

1. `npm run rhino:types` regenerates `src/types/rhino.d.ts` with one interface per registered server model.
2. `tsc --noEmit` (or the Vite build) passes; misusing a mutation variable (e.g. flat object to `useModelUpdate`) is a compile error.
3. Autocomplete on `response?.data[0].` offers the model's fields; all are optional.
4. `import type { PaginationMeta, LoginResult } from '@rhino-dev/rhino-react'` resolves.

## Agent: rhino-react/utilities

---
name: utilities
description: Rhino v4 client utilities expert. Use when configuring the API client (configureApi), calling custom non-CRUD endpoints via the exported api axios instance, using the storage/events adapters, extractPaginationFromHeaders, cn, or useToast.
model: inherit
---

You are the utilities specialist for the Rhino v4 React client (`@rhino-dev/rhino-react`).

## What this feature is

Beyond the CRUD hooks, the library exports the configured axios instance, platform-agnostic storage and event adapters, the pagination-header parser, a class-merging helper, and a headless toast hook. These are the escape hatches and plumbing — used for custom endpoints, cross-tab state, and non-web platforms.

## API surface

- **`configureApi(options)`** — configure the axios client once, before render. Options: `baseURL` (default `/api`), `tenancy: 'path' | 'subdomain'`, `routeGroup` (`string | null`, prefix-based groups only; auth URLs become `/{routeGroup}/auth/*`), `onUnauthorized: () => void` (401 — token already cleared), `onForbidden: (error) => void` (403 — token kept), `storage` adapter.
- **`api`** — the pre-configured axios instance all hooks use. `import { api } from '@rhino-dev/rhino-react'`. Automatically: attaches `Authorization: Bearer {token}` from storage, handles 401 (clear token + `onUnauthorized`) and 403 (`onForbidden`), sends credentials (`withCredentials: true`), sets JSON content type. Use it **ONLY for genuinely non-CRUD endpoints** (dashboards, reports, webhooks).
- **`storage`** — `getItem(key)` / `setItem(key, value)` / `removeItem(key)`. localStorage on web, AsyncStorage on React Native; swappable via `setStorageAdapter(adapter)` (or `configureApi({ storage })`; `setStorageAdapter(null)` resets). Internal keys: `token`, `organization_slug`, `route_group`. Also exported: `createWebStorage`, `initStorage`, `getStorageAdapter`, `StorageAdapter` type.
- **`events`** — `emit(key, value)` and `subscribe(key, cb) => unsubscribe`. Cross-tab on web via StorageEvent (org changes sync across tabs). Also `createWebEvents`.
- **`extractPaginationFromHeaders(response)`** — parses `X-Current-Page` / `X-Last-Page` / `X-Per-Page` / `X-Total` into `{ currentPage, lastPage, perPage, total }`; returns `null` if headers are absent. Used internally by every list hook; use it for custom paginated endpoints.
- **`cn(...classes)`** — clsx + tailwind-merge class merging (later conflicting Tailwind class wins).
- **`useToast()`** — `{ toasts, toast, dismiss }` headless in-memory toast state (`toast({ title, description, variant? })` returns `{ id }`). The canonical examples ship their own ToastProvider instead — no UI is included.
- Also exported: `buildAuthPath(action, routeGroup?)`, `getRouteGroup()`, `getTenancy()`, and the optional cogent-js `Query`/`loadCogent` URL builder.

## Code examples

```tsx
// Custom non-CRUD endpoint — wrap the api instance in your own useQuery/useMutation
import { api } from '@rhino-dev/rhino-react';
import { useQuery, useMutation } from '@tanstack/react-query';

const useDashboardStats = () =>
  useQuery({
    queryKey: ['dashboard-stats'],
    queryFn: async () => (await api.get('/dashboard/stats')).data,
  });

const useGenerateReport = () =>
  useMutation({
    mutationFn: (range: { startDate: string; endDate: string }) =>
      api.post('/reports/generate', range).then(r => r.data),
  });
```

```tsx
// Custom paginated endpoint using the header parser
import { api, extractPaginationFromHeaders } from '@rhino-dev/rhino-react';

const response = await api.get('/posts?page=2&per_page=15');
const pagination = extractPaginationFromHeaders(response);
// { currentPage: 2, lastPage: 10, perPage: 15, total: 143 } | null
```

```tsx
// Storage + cross-tab events
import { storage, events } from '@rhino-dev/rhino-react';

storage.setItem('theme', 'dark');
const theme = storage.getItem('theme');

const unsubscribe = events.subscribe('organization_slug', (newSlug) => {
  console.log('Organization changed in another tab:', newSlug);
});
// call unsubscribe() in the useEffect cleanup
```

```tsx
// Headless toast
import { useToast } from '@rhino-dev/rhino-react';

const { toast, dismiss, toasts } = useToast();
const { id } = toast({ title: 'Saved', description: 'Your changes have been saved.' });
setTimeout(() => dismiss(id), 3000);
// render `toasts` yourself — the library ships no toast UI
```

## Rules

- The `api` instance is for **non-CRUD only**. If the endpoint is a registered model's CRUD surface, use the model hooks — never hand-roll axios CRUD.
- Never read/write your own localStorage keys for auth/org state — the hooks read the `storage` adapter's `token` / `organization_slug` / `route_group` keys; go through `useAuth()` / `setOrganization()`.
- Wrap custom `api` calls in `useQuery`/`useMutation` so they get caching and status flags like everything else; pick query keys that don't collide with `['modelIndex', model]` / `['modelShow', model]`.
- `onUnauthorized` fires after the token is cleared (default redirects to `/`); `onForbidden` fires with the token kept — wire user-visible messaging there, not logout.
- On React Native, swap the storage adapter (AsyncStorage) and use `onUnauthorized` to navigate to the login screen instead of `window.location`.
- `cn` and `useToast` are the only UI-adjacent exports — v4 ships no component library.

## How to verify

1. A custom `api.get('/custom-endpoint')` request carries `Authorization: Bearer …` and `Accept: application/json` automatically.
2. Force a 401 (stale token): the token is cleared from storage and `onUnauthorized` runs; a 403 leaves the token in place and runs `onForbidden`.
3. Two browser tabs: changing the organization in one fires the `organization_slug` subscription in the other.
4. `extractPaginationFromHeaders` returns `null` for a response without the `X-*` headers and the full meta object when present.

## Skill: prd-codegen-contract

---
name: prd-codegen-contract
description: Cross-section PRD integrity rules for code generation. Use when reading prd.json, planning implementation, mapping roles/routes/features, or verifying codegen matches the PRD.
---

# PRD Codegen Contract

Enforce the same cross-section integrity rules used in PRD generation when planning and implementing Rhino applications. Read **`prd.json`** first; treat **`prd-integrity-report.json`** as the analysed-gaps output when present.

## Inputs

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `prd.json` | Structured PRD — single source of truth |
| `prd-integrity-report.json` | Analysed gaps / consistency violations from reflection or reconciler (optional but authoritative when present) |

## PRD sections codegen must use

- `application_roles` (or persona `application_roles`) — canonical role registry
- `key_features` — feature list for coverage matrix
- `user_flows` — flows with `primary_role`, `auth_required`, route-tagged steps when present
- `user_journeys` — persona scenarios tied to sitemap paths
- `user_personas` — map to application roles
- `sitemap` — route paths + `metadata.auth_required`, `metadata.allowed_roles`, `metadata.related_features`
- `navigation_design` — per-role entry points and menu items
- `user_stories` — `as_a`, acceptance criteria (especially ACL ACs)
- `phasing` — MVP vs later scope
- `planned_data_models` — use the **Rhino** slice only for implementation (generic is UX/documentation)
- `sample_data` — seed scenarios per role/persona

## Mandatory checks (before codegen or delegation)

1. **ROUTES:** Every navigation step in `user_flows` and `user_journeys` maps to a `sitemap` path (or is marked system/background).
2. **ROLES:** Personas, flow `primary_role`, journey actors, and `user_story.as_a` use the same role ids/labels as `application_roles` (no vague `"user"` when multiple roles exist).
3. **ACL:** Every protected sitemap node has matching auth ACs in stories; implement redirects/guards accordingly.
4. **FEATURES:** Every `key_feature` has ≥1 user flow, ≥1 journey (or explicit system-only), ≥1 user story, and ≥1 sitemap node (or admin surface).
5. **DATA:** Rhino `planned_data_models` and `sample_data` support fields collected in flows/stories; status enums match AC vocabulary.

## Gaps file handling

- **`status: unresolved` or `severity: blocker` conflicts** — do not delegate implementation; surface blockers to the user.
- **Resolved gaps** — document as explicit assumptions in the plan.
- If gaps file is missing, run the checklist above against `prd.json` yourself before planning.

## Feature Coverage Matrix (plan output)

For each `key_feature`, the implementation plan must trace:

| key_feature | user_flow(s) | user_journey(s) | sitemap route(s) | user_story(ies) | Rhino model(s) | specialists |

Each row becomes ordered tasks: roles → models → policies → auth routes → screens → integration.

## Role registry → Rhino

- `_roles.yaml` keys must match `application_roles` ids (map `administrator` → `admin` for org-admin gem behavior; see policy-specialist).
- Sitemap `metadata.allowed_roles` drives `RoleProtectedRoute` and policy scope.
- Every role in the registry needs seeds, policies, route group, and nav (see routing-specialist, shell-specialist).

## Data model rule

- Implement **`planned_data_models` Rhino definitions only** — not the generic UX-only model shown to users.
- Optional FKs (e.g. `user_id`) only when guest-access stories exist; required when all stories require login.
- Enum/status values must match acceptance criteria wording (e.g. Pending, Confirmed, Cancelled).

## When to use this skill

- **project-manager** — always, when building the implementation plan
- **policy-specialist**, **authentication-specialist**, **routing-specialist**, **shell-specialist** — roles, ACL, routes
- **data-model-specialist**, **model-specialist** — traceability and Rhino models
- **generated-ui-analyst**, **ui-integration-planner** — prototype vs sitemap drift
- **review-specialist** — PRD traceability pass before merge

## Skill: rhino-api-integration

---
name: rhino-api-integration
description: Integrate with Rhino backend APIs using core hooks directly. Use when fetching data, performing mutations, or managing API state. Use hooks from @rhino-project/core, NOT components from ui-heroui.
---

# Rhino API Integration

Integrate with Rhino backend APIs using `@rhino-project/core` hooks directly. Use hooks for data fetching and mutations, NOT components from `@rhino-project/ui-heroui`.

## When to Use

- Fetching data from Rhino API
- Creating, updating, or deleting resources
- Managing API state and cache
- Handling API errors

## Instructions

### Data Fetching

Use `useModelIndex` for lists:
```tsx
import { useModelIndex } from '@rhino-project/core/hooks';

const { resources, isPending, error } = useModelIndex('blog', {
  filters: { published: true },
  order: 'created_at',
  limit: 10,
  offset: 0
});
```

Use `useModelShow` for single resources:
```tsx
import { useModelShow } from '@rhino-project/core/hooks';

const { resource, isPending } = useModelShow('blog', blogId);
```

### Mutations

Create resources:
```tsx
import { useModelCreate } from '@rhino-project/core/hooks';

const { mutate: createBlog, isPending } = useModelCreate('blog', {
  onSuccess: () => {
    // Handle success
  },
  onError: (error) => {
    // Handle error
  }
});

// Call mutation
createBlog({ name: 'New Blog', organization_id: orgId });
```

Update resources:
```tsx
import { useModelUpdate } from '@rhino-project/core/hooks';

const { mutate: updateBlog, isPending } = useModelUpdate('blog', {
  onSuccess: () => {
    // Handle success
  }
});

updateBlog({ id: blogId, name: 'Updated Name' });
```

Delete resources:
```tsx
import { useModelDelete } from '@rhino-project/core/hooks';

const { mutate: deleteBlog, isPending } = useModelDelete('blog', {
  onSuccess: () => {
    // Handle success
  }
});

deleteBlog({ id: blogId });
```

### State Management

Use `useModelIndexController` for managing index state:
```tsx
import { useModelIndexController } from '@rhino-project/core/hooks';

const controller = useModelIndexController({
  model: 'blog',
  defaultFilter: { published: true }
});

// Access: controller.results, controller.filters, controller.setFilter, etc.
```

## Key Hooks

- `useModelIndex`: List resources
- `useModelShow`: Get single resource
- `useModelCreate`: Create resource
- `useModelUpdate`: Update resource
- `useModelDelete`: Delete resource
- `useModelIndexController`: Manage index state
- `useModelShowController`: Manage show/edit state

## Important Notes

- **DO NOT** use components from `@rhino-project/ui-heroui`
- **DO** use hooks from `@rhino-project/core/hooks`
- Build custom UI components that use these hooks
- Handle loading, error, and success states

Use the ask questions tool if you need to clarify API requirements, data structures, or error handling needs.

# Rhino Component

Create reusable React components with TypeScript types, props interfaces, and proper component structure using HeroUI.

## When to Use

- Creating reusable UI components

- Extracting common patterns into components
- Creating feature-specific components

## Instructions

### Step 1: Create Component File

Create component in `app/frontend/components/`:

```tsx
import { Card, CardBody, Button } from '@heroui/react';

interface BlogCardProps {
  blog: {
    id: number;
    name: string;
    description?: string;
  };
  onSelect?: (blog: { id: number; name: string }) => void;
}

export const BlogCard: React.FC<BlogCardProps> = ({ blog, onSelect }) => {
  return (
    <Card>
      <CardBody>
        <h3>{blog.name}</h3>
        {blog.description && <p>{blog.description}</p>}
        {onSelect && (
          <Button onClick={() => onSelect(blog)}>Select</Button>
        )}
      </CardBody>
    </Card>
  );
};
```

### Step 2: Define TypeScript Interfaces

- Define clear prop interfaces
- Use TypeScript for type safety

- Handle optional vs required props

### Step 3: Implement Component Logic

- Use React hooks appropriately
- Handle component state
- Implement event handlers
- Compose with HeroUI components

### Step 4: Export Component

- Export component properly
- Create index file if needed
- Document component usage

## Best Practices

- Single responsibility principle
- Composable and reusable
- Well-typed with TypeScript
- Accessible (ARIA attributes)

## Skill: rhino-debugging

---
name: rhino-debugging
description: Debug common Rhino issues. Use when troubleshooting errors, investigating bugs, or resolving issues in Rhino applications.
---

# Rhino Debugging

Troubleshooting guides and debugging patterns for common Rhino issues.

## When to Use

- Investigating errors or bugs
- Troubleshooting API issues
- Debugging authentication problems
- Resolving data fetching issues
- Fixing migration problems

## Common Issues & Solutions

### Backend Issues

**Model not appearing in API:**
- Check model is in `config/initializers/rhino.rb` resources array
- Verify `rhino_references` is configured
- Check `rhino_owner` is set correctly

**Migration errors:**
- Check migration syntax
- Verify foreign key constraints
- Ensure indexes are properly defined
- Check for migration conflicts

**Authentication issues:**
- Verify Devise Token Auth configuration
- Check token is being sent in headers
- Verify user is authenticated
- Check organization context

### Frontend Issues

**Data not loading:**
- Check `useModelIndex` or `useModelShow` hook usage
- Verify model name is correct
- Check network requests in browser dev tools
- Verify authentication token is present

**Hooks not working:**
- Check imports from `@rhino-project/core/hooks`
- Verify Rhino context is provided
- Check hook parameters are correct
- Verify model exists in resources
- **`useRoles()` returns `[]` despite seeded `UsersRole`:** component may be outside `BaseOwnerContext`. For flat-route apps, wrap shell with `AppOwnerProvider`; for TanStack apps, ensure component is inside `$owner` layout. Also verify seeds (see rhino-setup skill).

**Routing issues:**
- Check route file structure
- Verify route parameters
- Check authentication guards
- Verify route is in route tree
- **Nav click bounces to `/` (flat routes):** upstream `BaseOwnerProvider` inside `AuthenticatedRoute` redirects to `/<orgId>/` when `:baseOwnerId` is missing from the URL. Replace with `AppOwnerProvider` wrapping `Layout`; use `<Outlet />` only inside `AuthenticatedRoute`. See **routing-specialist**.
- **Wrong sidebar for signed-in role (e.g. admin sees couple nav):** shell/`Layout` renders outside `BaseOwnerContext` → `useRoles()` returns `[]`. Wrap the shell with `AppOwnerProvider` (flat routes) or ensure it is inside the `$owner` layout (TanStack).
- **`useBaseOwnerId()` is NaN:** flat URL scheme with owner-prefixed provider — same fix as above; org context must come from account data, not URL params.

## Debugging Steps

1. **Identify the Issue**
   - Read error messages carefully
   - Check browser console (frontend)
   - Check Rails logs (backend)
   - Check network tab for API calls

2. **Isolate the Problem**
   - Reproduce the issue
   - Identify what's working vs not working
   - Check related components/files
   - Verify configuration

3. **Check Common Causes**
   - Configuration issues
   - Missing dependencies
   - Incorrect hook usage
   - Authentication/authorization problems

4. **Apply Fix**
   - Fix the root cause
   - Test the fix
   - Verify related functionality still works

## Tools

- **Backend**: Rails console, logs, debugging gems
- **Frontend**: Browser dev tools, React DevTools, network tab
- **API**: Check network requests, verify responses

Use the ask questions tool if you need to clarify the issue, understand error messages, or get more context about the problem.

## Skill: rhino-frontend-page

---
name: rhino-frontend-page
description: Create new custom frontend pages with routing. Use when creating new pages that need custom routes, data fetching with Rhino hooks, and custom UI components. DO NOT use auto-generated model pages.

# Rhino Frontend Page

Create custom frontend pages with routing, data fetching using Rhino API hooks, and custom UI components. Do NOT use ModelIndexPage, ModelEditPage, ModelShowPage, or ModelCreatePage.

## When to Use

- Creating a new page/screen
- Building feature-specific pages
- Creating custom list or detail views
- Setting up new routes

## Instructions

### Step 1: Create Page Component

Create page component in `app/frontend/pages/`:

```tsx
import { useModelIndex } from '@rhino-project/core/hooks';
import { Container, Card, CardBody, Spinner } from '@heroui/react';

const BlogsPage = () => {
  const { resources, isPending, error } = useModelIndex('blog');
  
  if (isPending) return <Spinner />;
  if (error) return <div>Error loading blogs</div>;
  
  return (
    <Container>
      <h1>Blogs</h1>
      {resources?.results?.map(blog => (
        <Card key={blog.id}>
          <CardBody>{blog.name}</CardBody>
        </Card>
      ))}
    </Container>
  );
};

export default BlogsPage;
```

### Step 2: Create Route File

Create route file in `app/frontend/routes/` following the app's router and URL scheme:

**TanStack (owner-prefixed):**
```tsx
import { createFileRoute } from '@tanstack/react-router';
import BlogsPage from '../../pages/BlogsPage';

export const Route = createFileRoute('/_authenticated/$owner/blogs/')({
  component: BlogsPage
});
```

**React Router flat paths (`custom.jsx`):** add a `<Route path="/blogs" element={…} />` entry inside `customRoutes()`; ensure `Layout` is wrapped with `AppOwnerProvider` (see **routing-specialist**).

### Step 3: Use Appropriate Rhino Hooks

- **List/Index pages**: Use `useModelIndex` with filters, pagination, sorting
- **Detail/Show pages**: Use `useModelShow` with resource ID
- **Create forms**: Use `useModelCreate` hook
- **Edit forms**: Use `useModelShow` to fetch + `useModelUpdate` to save

### Step 4: Build Custom UI

- Use HeroUI components for UI
- Handle loading states
- Handle error states
- Handle empty states
- Build custom layouts

## Important Notes

- **DO NOT** use ModelIndexPage, ModelEditPage, ModelShowPage, ModelCreatePage
- **DO** build custom pages using Rhino hooks and HeroUI
- All routes should be custom and feature-specific
- Use `useModelIndex`, `useModelShow`, `useModelCreate`, `useModelUpdate`, `useModelDelete` hooks

Use the ask questions tool if you need to clarify page requirements, data fetching needs, or UI design.

## Skill: rhino-migration

---
name: rhino-migration
description: Create and manage database migrations following Rhino conventions. Use when modifying database schema, adding columns, creating tables, or handling data migrations.
---

# Rhino Migration

Create and manage database migrations following Rhino conventions with proper indexes, foreign keys, and constraints.

## When to Use

- Creating new tables
- Adding or modifying columns
- Adding indexes
- Creating foreign key constraints
- Performing data migrations

## Instructions

### Creating a Migration

1. **Generate Migration**
   ```bash
   rails generate migration MigrationName
   ```

2. **Add Schema Changes**
   - Use appropriate migration methods
   - Add columns with proper types
   - Add foreign keys with `references` or `add_foreign_key`
   - Add indexes for foreign keys and frequently queried columns

3. **Follow Rhino Patterns**
   - Add `organization_id` foreign key for organization-owned resources
   - Include `created_at` and `updated_at` timestamps
   - Add indexes on foreign keys
   - Consider soft deletes with `deleted_at` if needed

### Example Migration

```ruby
class CreateBlogs < ActiveRecord::Migration[8.0]
  def change
    create_table :blogs do |t|
      t.references :organization, null: false, foreign_key: true
      t.string :name, null: false
      t.text :description
      t.timestamps
    end
    
    add_index :blogs, :organization_id
    add_index :blogs, :name
  end
end
```

### Adding Columns

```ruby
class AddPublishedToPosts < ActiveRecord::Migration[8.0]
  def change
    add_column :posts, :published, :boolean, default: false, null: false
    add_index :posts, :published
  end
end
```

### Data Migrations

For data migrations, use `up` and `down` methods:
```ruby
class MigratePostStatus < ActiveRecord::Migration[8.0]
  def up
    Post.where(status: 'active').update_all(published: true)
  end
  
  def down
    Post.where(published: true).update_all(status: 'active')
  end
end
```

## Best Practices

- Always test migrations in development first
- Use transactions for data migrations when possible
- Add indexes for foreign keys and frequently queried columns
- Consider production downtime for large migrations
- Keep migrations focused and atomic
- Test rollback procedures

Use the ask questions tool if you need to clarify migration requirements, data safety concerns, or rollback strategies.

## Skill: rhino-model-creation

---
name: rhino-model-creation
description: Create a new Rhino model with all required setup. Use when creating a new model that needs migration, rhino configuration, validations, associations, and admin interface.
---

# Rhino Model Creation

Create a complete Rhino model with all required setup including model file, migration, Rhino configuration, validations, associations, and admin interface.

## When to Use

- Creating a new model from scratch
- Adding a new resource to the Rhino API
- Setting up a new database entity

## Instructions

Before creating models, read **`prd.json`** (Rhino `planned_data_models` only) and the **`prd-codegen-contract` skill**. Document which user stories/flows each model supports; match enums and nullability to acceptance criteria.

### Step 1: Create Model File

Create the model in `app/models/` with:
- Proper class name (singular, PascalCase)
- Inherit from `ApplicationRecord` or Rhino base class
- Add `rhino_owner` for multi-tenancy
- Add `rhino_references` for API exposure
- Add validations
- Define associations

Example:
```ruby
# frozen_string_literal: true

class Blog < ApplicationRecord
  belongs_to :organization
  has_many :posts, dependent: :destroy
  
  rhino_owner :organization
  rhino_references [:organization]
  
  validates :name, presence: true
  validates :organization, presence: true
end
```

### Step 2: Create Migration

Generate migration:
```bash
rails generate migration CreateBlogs organization:references name:string
```

Add to migration:
- Foreign key constraints
- Indexes on foreign keys
- Indexes on frequently queried columns
- Timestamps

### Step 3: Add to Rhino Resources

Add model to `config/initializers/rhino.rb`:
```ruby
config.resources += ["Blog"]
```

### Step 4: Create Admin Interface (Optional)

Create `app/admin/blog.rb`:
```ruby
ActiveAdmin.register Blog do
  # Admin configuration
end
```

### Step 5: Run Migration

```bash
rails db:migrate
```

### Step 6: Verify

- Check model file is correct
- Verify migration ran successfully
- Check model is in resources array
- Test model in console

Use the ask questions tool if you need to clarify model requirements, relationships, or ownership structure.

## Skill: rhino-setup

---
name: rhino-setup
description: Initialize new Rhino features or components. Use when setting up new models, migrations, frontend pages, or other Rhino components from scratch. ALWAYS set up multi-tenancy with organizations first for new projects.
---

# Rhino Setup

Complete setup workflows for common Rhino tasks including models, migrations, frontend pages, and component initialization.

## When to Use

- Setting up a new Rhino project from scratch
- Initial project setup with multi-tenancy
- Setting up a new feature from scratch
- Initializing new models and their related components
- Creating new frontend pages and routes
- Setting up new components or utilities

## Instructions

### Initial Project Setup (ALWAYS FIRST - Multi-Tenancy Required)

**IMPORTANT**: Multi-tenancy with organizations is ALWAYS used in Rhino projects. This setup MUST be completed before any other model or feature setup.

1. **Install Organizations Engine**
   - Run the organizations install command: `rails rhino_organizations:install` (or equivalent generator)
   - This installs the organizations engine migrations and sets up the multi-tenancy foundation
   - Ensures the `rhino_project_organizations` gem is properly configured

2. **Run Migrations**
   - Run all migrations: `rails db:migrate`
   - This creates the organizations, users_roles, roles, and users_role_invites tables
   - Sets up the database schema for multi-tenancy

3. **Run Seeds — and seed the PRD's roles + assign them**
   - Run seeds: `rails db:seed`
   - Read **`prd.json`** (`application_roles`) and the **`prd-codegen-contract` skill**. `_roles.yaml` keys must match the PRD role registry.
   - The default seeds create baseline organizations/roles/users_roles. **For a role-based app you MUST also seed the PRD's own roles and assign them**, or role-based access silently fails everywhere:
     - Create a `Role` row for every key in `.agentcode/blueprints/_roles.yaml` (e.g. `Role.find_or_create_by!(name: 'patient')`). Use `admin` (not `administrator`) for the privileged role — the organizations gem hardcodes the literal `admin` for membership/settings checks (see policy-specialist).
     - Give each demo/test user a `UsersRole` (user + organization + role). **Without a `UsersRole`, `useRoles()` returns `[]` and every backend policy AND every frontend role gate denies — the user appears locked out of every page.** This is the #1 cause of "the physician role can't access anything."
     - Seed **`sample_data`** from `prd.json` when present — at least one row per primary persona scenario with FKs matching journey narratives.
   - Verify after seeding: `rails runner "u=User.first; puts UsersRole.where(user: u).joins(:role).pluck('roles.name').inspect"` should print the expected role(s), not `[]`.

4. **Configure Rhino Base Owner**
   - Ensure `config/initializers/rhino.rb` has `config.base_owner = 'Organization'` uncommented
   - This enables organization-based multi-tenancy

**This initial setup must be completed before proceeding with any other setup tasks.**

### Backend Setup (After Initial Project Setup)

1. **Model Setup**
   - Create model file in `app/models/`
   - Add `rhino_owner` configuration
   - Add `rhino_references` for API exposure
   - Add validations and associations
   - Add model to `config/initializers/rhino.rb` resources array

2. **Migration Setup**
   - Generate migration with proper naming
   - Add columns with appropriate types
   - Add foreign key constraints
   - Add indexes for foreign keys and frequently queried columns
   - Run migration and verify schema

3. **Admin Setup** (if needed)
   - Create admin file in `app/admin/`
   - Configure ActiveAdmin resource
   - Add admin-specific functionality

### Frontend Setup

1. **Page Setup**
   - Create page component in `app/frontend/pages/`
   - Create route file in `app/frontend/routes/`
   - Use Rhino API hooks for data fetching
   - Build custom UI with HeroUI components

2. **Component Setup**
   - Create component in `app/frontend/components/`
   - Define TypeScript interfaces for props
   - Implement component logic
   - Add tests if needed

## Steps

### For New Projects:
1. **ALWAYS FIRST**: Set up multi-tenancy with organizations (install, migrate, seed)
2. Configure Rhino base owner in `config/initializers/rhino.rb`
3. Then proceed with feature setup

### For New Features:
1. Identify what needs to be set up (model, page, component, etc.)
2. Create necessary files following Rhino conventions
3. Configure Rhino-specific settings (rhino_owner, rhino_references, resources)
4. Add to appropriate configuration files
5. Test the setup

Use the ask questions tool if you need to clarify requirements or if any step is unclear.

## Skill: rhino-shell-creation

---
name: rhino-shell-creation
description: Create custom application shell to replace ApplicationShell. Use when building custom shell components with navigation, layout, and authentication integration.
---

# Rhino Shell Creation

Create custom application shell component to replace ApplicationShell from ui-heroui. Build custom shell with navigation, layout, and authentication integration.

## When to Use

- Replacing ApplicationShell with custom implementation
- Building custom navigation structures
- Creating application layouts
- Integrating authentication and organization context

## Instructions

### Step 1: Create Shell Component

Create shell component in `app/frontend/components/app/ApplicationShell.tsx`:

```tsx
import { useUser, useBaseOwnerId } from '@rhino-project/core/hooks';
import { useRhinoContext } from '@rhino-project/core';
import { Navbar, NavbarContent, NavbarItem, Container } from '@heroui/react';

interface ApplicationShellProps {
  children: React.ReactNode;
}

export const ApplicationShell: React.FC<ApplicationShellProps> = ({ children }) => {
  const user = useUser();
  const baseOwnerId = useBaseOwnerId();
  const { rhino } = useRhinoContext();
  
  return (
    <div className="min-h-screen flex flex-col">
      <Navbar>
        <NavbarContent>
          <NavbarItem>Logo</NavbarItem>
          {/* Add navigation items */}
        </NavbarContent>
        <NavbarContent justify="end">
          <NavbarItem>{user?.name || user?.email}</NavbarItem>
        </NavbarContent>
      </Navbar>
      <main className="flex-1">
        <Container>
          {children}
        </Container>
      </main>
    </div>
  );
};
```

### Step 2: Create Navigation Components

Create primary and secondary navigation components:

```tsx
// PrimaryNavigation.tsx
export const PrimaryNavigation = () => {
  return (
    <nav>
      {/* Navigation items */}
    </nav>
  );
};
```

### Step 3: Integrate with Rhino Context

- Use `useUser()` for current user
- Use `useBaseOwnerId()` for current organization (works once `BaseOwnerContext` is provided)
- Use `useRhinoContext()` for Rhino context
- Handle organization switching if needed
- **Wrap the shell with `AppOwnerProvider`** when the app uses flat routes (`custom.jsx`) — see **routing-specialist**. Without this, `useRoles()` in the sidebar returns `[]` and nav shows the wrong role.
- **Do not use upstream `BaseOwnerProvider` with flat routes** — it redirects to `/<orgId>/` and causes nav bounces to `/`.

### Step 4: Replace ApplicationShell Usage

**TanStack owner-prefixed routes:**
```tsx
import { ApplicationShell } from '../../components/app/ApplicationShell';

function RouteComponent() {
  return (
    <ApplicationShell>
      <Outlet />
    </ApplicationShell>
  );
}
```

**Flat routes (`custom.jsx`):** wrap shell with `AppOwnerProvider` in the route tree (see **routing-specialist**). Do not nest upstream `BaseOwnerProvider` inside `AuthenticatedRoute`.

## Important Notes

- **DO NOT** use ApplicationShell from `@rhino-project/ui-heroui`
- **DO** build completely custom shell component
- Integrate with Rhino context for authentication and organization
- Use HeroUI components for shell UI
- **Shell/sidebar calling `useRoles()` must render inside `AppOwnerProvider`** (flat routes) or the `$owner` layout (TanStack) — otherwise nav shows the wrong role

Use the ask questions tool if you need to clarify shell requirements, navigation structure, or layout design.

## Skill: rhino-testing

---
name: rhino-testing
description: Set up tests for Rhino features. Use when creating test files for models, controllers, frontend components, or setting up test infrastructure.
---

# Rhino Testing

Set up tests for Rhino features including backend tests (Minitest), frontend tests (Vitest), and end-to-end tests (Cypress).

## When to Use

- Writing tests for new features
- Setting up test infrastructure
- Creating test fixtures or factories
- Ensuring test coverage

## Instructions

### Backend Tests (Minitest)

**Model Tests** (`test/models/blog_test.rb`):
```ruby
require 'test_helper'

class BlogTest < ActiveSupport::TestCase
  test "should require name" do
    blog = Blog.new
    assert_not blog.valid?
    assert_includes blog.errors[:name], "can't be blank"
  end
  
  test "should belong to organization" do
    blog = blogs(:one)
    assert_not_nil blog.organization
  end
end
```

**Controller Tests** (`test/controllers/blogs_controller_test.rb`):
```ruby
require 'test_helper'

class BlogsControllerTest < ActionDispatch::IntegrationTest
  test "should get index" do
    get api_blogs_url, headers: auth_headers
    assert_response :success
  end
end
```

### Frontend Tests (Vitest)

**Component Tests** (`app/frontend/__tests__/BlogList.spec.tsx`):
```tsx
import { render, screen } from '@testing-library/react';
import { BlogList } from '../BlogList';

vi.mock('@rhino-project/core/hooks', () => ({
  useModelIndex: () => ({
    resources: { results: [{ id: 1, name: 'Test Blog' }] },
    isPending: false
  })
}));

describe('BlogList', () => {
  it('renders blogs', () => {
    render(<BlogList />);
    expect(screen.getByText('Test Blog')).toBeInTheDocument();
  });
});
```

### E2E Tests (Cypress)

**Feature Tests** (`cypress/e2e/blogs.cy.js`):
```javascript
describe('Blogs', () => {
  it('should create a blog', () => {
    cy.visit('/blogs');
    cy.get('[data-testid="create-blog"]').click();
    cy.get('input[name="name"]').type('New Blog');
    cy.get('button[type="submit"]').click();
    cy.contains('New Blog').should('be.visible');
  });
});
```

## Test Setup

1. Create test file in appropriate directory
2. Set up test data (factories, fixtures, mocks)
3. Write test cases
4. Run tests and verify they pass
5. Ensure adequate coverage

Use the ask questions tool if you need to clarify testing requirements, test scenarios, or test data setup.
